title: ELMo: Context from LSTMs
concept: ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models) used a deep bidirectional LSTM to generate word vectors, showing that context dramatically improves downstream tasks.

exercises.prompt: Which neural network architecture does ELMo rely on to capture sentence context?
exercises.explain: ELMo stands for 'Embeddings from Language Models' and is famous for using LSTMs to create context-aware word vectors.

cards.title: One Word, Many Meanings
cards.body: In early AI, the word 'bank' always had the same math representation, whether you were talking about a river bank or a money bank. How did AI finally learn to read between the lines? Enter ELMo!

cards.title: What is ELMo?
cards.body: ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models) is an AI model that looks at an entire sentence using a two-way (bidirectional) LSTM. Instead of assigning a fixed definition to a word, ELMo creates custom word vectors on the fly based on the words around them.

cards.title: The Wardrobe Department
cards.body: Imagine words are actors. Traditional word vectors are like giving an actor one costume they must wear in every single movie. ELMo is like a backstage wardrobe department: it checks the script (the surrounding sentence) and hands the actor the perfect costume for that specific scene.

cards.title: Context in Action
cards.body: Consider the word 'apple'. In 'I ate a juicy apple', ELMo creates a vector representing fruit. In 'I bought a new Apple laptop', ELMo looks at 'laptop' and adjusts the vector to represent the technology company.

cards.title: Not Just a Dictionary
cards.body: People think that ELMo is just a dictionary lookup. In reality, ELMo doesn't use static lookups. It runs the entire sentence through a deep LSTM neural network to calculate a fresh, dynamic vector for every word, every single time.

cards.title: Static vs. Dynamic Context
cards.body: Let's compare how old static embeddings look at words versus how ELMo's dynamic contextual embeddings look at them.

left.text: One fixed vector per word. 'Play' (the game) and 'Play' (the theater show) look identical to the computer.

right.text: Dynamic vectors. The vector for 'Play' changes depending on whether the nearby words are 'game' or 'theater'.

exercises.prompt: What was the main limitation of older word embeddings before ELMo?

options.text: They were much larger in size.
They assigned the exact same vector to a word regardless of its context.
They were trained on audio files instead of text.

exercises.explain: Before ELMo, models like Word2Vec assigned one fixed vector to each word, meaning 'bank' (river) and 'bank' (money) got the exact same representation. ELMo solved this by making vectors context-dependent.

exercises.prompt: ELMo reads text both forward and backward to understand the context of a word.
exercises.explain: Yes! ELMo uses a bidirectional LSTM, meaning it processes text both forward (left-to-right) and backward (right-to-left) to capture full context.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe ELMo's two-way reading direction.
exercises.sentence: ELMo reads sentences in both directions using a [blank] LSTM network.
exercises.explain: ELMo uses a bidirectional LSTM to read sentences forward and backward, allowing it to gather complete context.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes how ELMo generates its word vectors?

options.text: It uses a simple dictionary lookup.
It generates word vectors dynamically using the surrounding context.
It ignores the context of the sentence entirely.

exercises.explain: ELMo stands out because it dynamically calculates word representations based on the surrounding sentence, rather than looking up fixed values from a table.

exercises.prompt: If you pass the sentences 'I go for a run' and 'Water pipes run cold' into ELMo, how does it handle the word 'run'?

options.text: ELMo looks at the surrounding context in both directions to give the word 'run' a different vector based on its usage.
ELMo looks up 'run' in a pre-made dictionary and uses the exact same vector every time.
ELMo deletes the word 'run' and replaces it with a generic placeholder vector.
ELMo only reads sentences from right to left, ignoring the start of the sentence.

exercises.explain: ELMo generates context-sensitive embeddings by looking at the whole sentence in both directions using LSTMs, which allows it to distinguish between homographs like the verb 'run' and the noun 'run'. Traditional Word2Vec only gives a single static vector per word regardless of context.
